title: Former Abortion Industry Worker Exposes Ft. Worth Clinic and the Truth About Pro-Life Regulations
author: James Silberman
orientation: An Apologia Studios interview with Amanda, a former Whole Women's Health employee in Ft. Worth, surfaces internal practices at the facility (a surgical-abortion quota, ignored sonogram and reporting requirements, and unused fetal dopplers) and is presented by Free the States as evidence that pro-life regulations are unenforceable and that abolition, not regulation, must be the demand.
audiences: Christian considering sidewalk ministry or evangelism

questions.question: What surprising internal policy did Amanda say Whole Women's Health imposes on its abortionist, and why does it matter that the price is the same?
questions.answer: Amanda revealed the facility imposes a surgical-abortion quota even though surgical and chemical abortions are billed identically, which removes the obvious profit motive and raises the question of why surgical procedures are being preferred at all.
questions.quote: They're not going to make more money off someone doing surgical versus the medication because they're the same price... They push the surgical because apparently there's like a quota he has to meet for the surgicals themselves, not so much for the medication but for the surgical… I'm not sure why, I never was told why. I just was told that 'we really need to push surgicals.'

questions.question: What back-end revenue stream do Speed and Ten Bruggencate suspect could explain the surgical quota?
questions.answer: They suggest the only plausible reason for a quota when prices are equal is profit on the back end of the slaughter, namely sale of body parts to biomedical researchers, citing David Daleiden's investigative work.
questions.quote: As David Daleiden pointed out in his work, these aborted babies are likely being sold to researchers.

questions.question: How does the clinic appear to be handling the Texas sonogram law in practice?
questions.answer: Amanda said the facility only provides the legally required sonogram if the mother explicitly asks, treating the law as if mothers had given 'implied consent' to skip it, which inverts the statute's actual requirement that a sonogram be offered with a right to decline.
questions.quote: Speed told Free the States that Amanda's explanation of the employees' understanding of the policy is that they were operating on the principle of "implied consent," meaning that if the mother didn't ask to see the sonogram, that signaled that she didn't want to see the sonogram. This understanding is not consistent with state law which mandates that the mother be offered the sonogram with the ability to decline if she wants to.

questions.question: What did Amanda observe about the fetal dopplers the law requires the clinic to use?
questions.answer: She said the fetal dopplers had never been used in her time there and were still in their original packaging, indicating the heartbeat-display requirement was not being honored at all.
questions.quote: They're still in the box as far as the last time I checked, because I have never seen anyone bust them out of the box. Like, brand spanking new. Brand new, still in the box – I think it still has the paper stuff wrapped around it.

questions.question: How does the clinic's abortionist allegedly evade the injury-reporting requirement?
questions.answer: Amanda said the abortionist files a report only when he 'knows' he caused an injury and not when he merely 'suspects' he did, exploiting the language of the regulation to avoid documenting harms he caused during procedures.
questions.quote: Yeah. As long as he suspects. Unless he knows-knows, then they have to file a report. But if he says he suspects it, then they don't have to.

questions.question: What does Speed argue is the lesson abolitionists and pro-lifers should draw from this clinic's behavior?
questions.answer: Speed argues that the disregard for regulations proves they do nothing to slow the killing and primarily serve to keep the pro-life movement's voters and donors engaged rather than actually ending abortion.
questions.quote: The lack of regard for regulations at the clinic just points out how incredibly meaningless they are. They're not doing anything to slow down the slaughter at Whole Woman's Health. They do more to secure the pro-life voting and donation base than they could ever do to end abortion.

questions.question: Whom does Speed blame for the continued killing at the Ft. Worth clinic, and what specific legislative action does he cite?
questions.answer: Speed blames Texas pro-life political apathy and specifically Rep. Jeff Leach's killing of HB896, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act, in favor of a born-alive protection act, leaving the busiest WWH office free to perform 500 abortions in June alone.
questions.quote: The Fort Worth office is the busiest office that Whole Women's Health has. They did 500 abortions in June alone. We often see as many as 60 cars on a Saturday and there are more than that during the week. It continues in Texas precisely because [Rep. Jeff Leach killed TX HB896 [The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act] and put forward a born alive protection act].

questions.question: What psychological tactic does Ten Bruggencate think the clinic uses to keep its staff working there?
questions.answer: He suggests managers deliberately insulate most staff from the gruesome reality of the killing room so workers can continue without confronting what they are participating in, even though they know it intellectually.
questions.quote: What I thought was interesting too is that they insulate the staff from [abortion]. There are only a very few people who get to see the gruesome part of it. I think that's to insulate them from what they're actually doing. Although they know it, but I think it makes it even more real to them when they see that.

questions.question: Why did Amanda actually quit, and how did Ten Bruggencate respond to her motivation?
questions.audience: Christian considering sidewalk ministry or evangelism
questions.answer: Amanda quit for practical reasons — her employer forced her to choose between the job and her small child — and Ten Bruggencate noted that while they celebrate her leaving, the goal of ministry is repentance and trust in Christ, not merely getting workers to walk away.
questions.quote: We're thrilled that she quit her job, but if she doesn't repent and put her trust in Jesus Christ, she's no closer to heaven for quitting her job. So our goal is not to get women to quit their jobs. It's not to get women to stop killing their babies. Ultimately, we want that, but we want it because we want them to be saved.

questions.question: How did Amanda first connect with Jon Speed, and what does that say about the effect of explicitly Christian sidewalk ministry?
questions.audience: Christian considering sidewalk ministry or evangelism
questions.answer: Amanda contacted Speed by Facebook Messenger the day she quit because the clinic workers regularly watched his livestreams from their facility, suggesting that explicit Gospel-centered outreach actually draws workers rather than scaring them away as many pro-life groups assume.
questions.quote: She decided to reach out to me because the workers in the clinic watch my livestreams when I am there doing ministry. She mentioned that the workers would sometimes chat about what they would do if they left the clinic and most say that they would 'talk to the people on the other side of the fence.' She reached out to me the day she quit. My wife and I met her formally the following Tuesday for lunch.

questions.question: How did Speed and his church practically support Amanda after she quit?
questions.audience: Christian considering sidewalk ministry or evangelism
questions.answer: They helped her find gig work, connected her to a church member helping her pursue a medical-assistant job, set up an Amazon registry for her kids' back-to-school needs that sold out in hours, and raised over $3,000 in about a week toward her one-month $2,100 expense goal.
questions.quote: She's found some gig work to do on the weekends. A lady in our church is working with her to try to find a job in the medical field as a medical assistant. We put together an Amazon gift registry for her kids for back to school stuff and it sold out within a few hours. We had a goal to get her $2,100 to cover one month of expenses while she looks for work. We raised a little more than $3,000 in about a week through my social media platform and our church.

questions.question: How does End Abortion Now's stated mission differ from mainstream pro-life strategy?
questions.answer: EAN explicitly rejects neutrality and incrementalism, asserting that the church must fight abortion on the basis of Scripture and pursue equal-protection legislation that criminalizes abortion as murder rather than negotiating with it.
questions.quote: We believe that it is the responsibility of the Church to fight abortion as Christians who stand on the Word of God. It is our humble assertion that the modern day Pro-Life movement has failed to end abortion because they have abandoned this sure foundation and elected to fight on the basis of neutrality rather than Scripture.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Directly cited in the article as the explanation for why HB896 died in Texas under Rep. Jeff Leach.

related.title: Pro-Life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence to 110,000 Preborn Human Beings
related.reason: Names the legislator the article blames for keeping Whole Women's Health open in Texas.

related.title: 110,000 Lives Hang In the Balance As Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act Gets April 8th Hearing
related.reason: Background on HB896, the Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act referenced by Speed as the alternative to ineffective regulations.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Develops the broader argument the interview illustrates: that incremental pro-life regulations are ineffective and counterproductive.

related.title: Moving Beyond Regulationism: A Response to David Norman
related.reason: Theological and strategic case against treating abortion as something to be regulated rather than abolished, matching this article's 'Abolition, Not Regulation' thesis.

related.title: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
related.reason: Provides the judicial backdrop to Speed's claim that pro-life regulations are 'unenforceable' loopholes.

related.title: Christian, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Echoes Ten Bruggencate's emphasis that Gospel proclamation, not merely getting workers or mothers to stop, is the heart of the abolitionist mission.
